name: Mirandese

ancestors.notes: Old Portuguese (Aviz-period) adstrate: as Portuguese consolidated as the state language, the Leonese-speaking communities of Miranda, Rionor, and Guadramil faced intensifying pressure. Portuguese orthographic conventions (lh, nh, ã) penetrated the writing tradition.

notes: Mirandese (mirandés), spoken in the Miranda do Douro municipality, northeastern Portugal. Classified as Asturleonese. Recognised as a regional/minority language in Portugal since 1999. Retains archaic Asturleonese features: diphthongisation of Latin short /e/ and /o/, palatal lateral /ʎ/ preserved, nasal vowels influenced by Portuguese orthographic proximity. Heavy Portuguese adstrate via official education and administration. Mirandese preserves the full medieval six-sibilant contrast: apical /s̺ z̺/ (⟨s⟩, intervocalic ⟨-s-⟩), laminal /s̻ z̻/ (⟨c, ç⟩, ⟨z⟩), postalveolar /ʃ ʒ/ (⟨x⟩, ⟨j, g⟩). Hence ⟨j⟩ is /ʒ/ (western Iberian, never the Asturian palatal ʝ) and ⟨ç⟩ is the voiceless laminal fricative /s̻/, not an affricate; ⟨ç⟩ also occurs word-finally (rapaç, lhuç) where Portuguese writes -z.

sources.author: Vasconcelos, J. Leite de
sources.title: Estudos de Philologia Mirandesa
sources.notes: Foundational description of Mirandese phonology, including the six-sibilant system.

sources.author: Ferreira, M.B. & Raposo, D. (coords.)
sources.title: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa
sources.publisher: Câmara Municipal de Miranda do Douro / Centro de Linguística da Universidade de Lisboa
sources.notes: Official orthography: grapheme-phoneme conventions and Portuguese-based accentuation rules.

stress.notes: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa (1999) accentuation, Portuguese-based: paroxytone default; written accent (acute or tilde) overrides. Oxytone-attracting endings reflect Mirandese orthography: consonant-final r/l/z/ç (rapaç, Pertual, falar) and the final nasals written with -n in the Asturleonese manner — -in (camin, Sendin), -un, and the systematically oxytone -on class from Latin -ōnem (naçon, coraçon, Associaçon). Unmarked -an/-en verb endings stay paroxytone as in Portuguese -am/-em.
